schema.description: Each card has a 'type' field and optional fields depending on type.

types.fill-in: Sentence with a blank to fill (front=sentence with ___, back=answer, hint=optional clue)
types.translate: Full sentence translation (front=English or Japanese sentence, back=translation)

meta.notes: Japanese uses three scripts: Hiragana (native words), Katakana (foreign words), and Kanji (Chinese-origin characters). Hiragana and Katakana each have 46 base characters. Master pronunciation rules before moving on — long vowels, double consonants, and pitch accent are commonly overlooked early.
cheatSheet: Hiragana: native Japanese words and grammar particles
Katakana: foreign loanwords, emphasis, onomatopoeia
Kanji: Chinese-origin characters with multiple readings (on-yomi & kun-yomi)
っ (small tsu) = double the next consonant (e.g. きって = kit-te)
Long vowels: aa, ii, uu, ei, ou — hold twice as long
ん (n) sounds like 'm' before b/m/p, 'ng' before k/g
tips: Do not try to memorize all kana at once — learn one row at a time
Practice writing each character by hand to build muscle memory
Use the Kana practice section for dedicated hiragana/katakana drilling
Focus on pronunciation rules before moving to vocabulary

cards.back: Extend vowel duration. aa, ii, uu, ei/ee, ou/oo. In hiragana: おかあさん (okaasan), おにいさん (oniisan). For 'oo', write おう (e.g., おはよう).
Pronounced 'm' before b/m/p (e.g., さんぽ = sampo). Pronounced 'ng' before k/g (e.g., まんが = manga). Otherwise 'n'.
The 'u' in です (desu) and ます (masu) is often silent. The 'i' in すき (suki) can be devoiced between voiceless consonants.
Japanese uses pitch (high/low) rather than stress (loud/soft). はし can mean 'bridge' (LH) or 'chopsticks' (HL) depending on pitch.
Small tsu (っ) creates a pause/glottal stop before the next consonant. きって (kitte) = stamp. がっこう (gakkou) = school.
Combine i-column kana with small ya/yu/yo: きゃ (kya), しゅ (shu), ちょ (cho). The ya/yu/yo must be written small.
Dakuten voices consonants: k→g, s→z, t→d, h→b. Handakuten: h→p. E.g., か→が, さ→ざ, は→ば, は→ぱ.
Used for: foreign loanwords (コーヒー), foreign names (メアリー), onomatopoeia, emphasis, and scientific terms.
Use ー (long dash) instead of vowel kana: コーヒー (koohii), ケーキ (keeki), ノート (nooto).
For sounds not native to Japanese: ティ (ti), ディ (di), ファ (fa), フェ (fe), フィ (fi), ヴ (vu).
Characters derived from pictures of objects. 木 (tree), 山 (mountain), 川 (river), 日 (sun).

cards.front: Kanji Formation: Simple Ideograms (指事文字)
cards.back: Abstract concepts shown with symbols. 上 (above), 下 (below), 一 (one), 二 (two), 三 (three).

cards.front: Kanji Formation: Compound Ideograms (会意文字)
cards.back: Two or more elements combined for meaning. 明 (bright) = 日 (sun) + 月 (moon). 休 (rest) = 人 (person) + 木 (tree).
On-yomi: Chinese-derived reading, used in compound words (e.g., 山 = サン in 火山/kazan). Kun-yomi: native Japanese reading, used alone (e.g., 山 = やま).

cards.front: 明 combines 日 (___) and 月 (___) to mean 'bright'

meta.notes: Japanese greetings are highly context-dependent — time of day, social setting, and your relationship with the person all shape which form to use. Formal and informal registers differ significantly. Learn the situations first, then the words.
cheatSheet: おはようございます (ohayou gozaimasu) = Good morning (formal)
こんにちは (konnichiwa) = Hello / Good afternoon
こんばんは (konbanwa) = Good evening
ありがとうございます (arigatou gozaimasu) = Thank you (formal)
すみません (sumimasen) = Excuse me / I'm sorry
よろしくおねがいします = Nice to meet you / Please treat me well
tips: Japanese greetings change based on the time of day and formality level
おはよう (ohayou) is casual; おはようございます is polite — know your audience
Bowing is often paired with greetings — depth of bow indicates respect level
Practice saying these aloud, not just reading them

cards.front: おはよう ございます。
It's 8 AM. You greet your teacher: ＿＿＿＿ ございます。
It's 3 PM. You meet a friend: ＿＿＿＿。

cards.back: I'll go and come back. (said when leaving home)
Please go and come back. (response to above)
Thank you for the meal. (before eating)
Thank you for the meal. (after eating)

cards.front: You're about to eat dinner. You say: ＿＿＿＿。
You just got home. You say: ＿＿＿＿。 Your family responds: ＿＿＿＿。
cards.back: ただいま / おかえり

cards.front: ありがとう ございます。
よろしく おねがいします。

cards.back: 1. Excuse me (getting attention). 2. I'm sorry (apology). 3. Thank you (for trouble caused). Context determines meaning.

cards.front: How do you do? I am Tanaka. Nice to meet you.
cards.back: はじめまして。たなかです。よろしくおねがいします。
Bowing is the standard Japanese greeting. Deeper bows show more respect. A casual nod (15°) for friends, 30° for business, 45° for deep apologies.
Exchange with both hands. Receive and read it carefully. Never write on someone's card or put it in your back pocket.

meta.notes: Japanese counting uses native Japanese numbers (ひとつ、ふたつ…) for objects 1–10 and Sino-Japanese numbers (いち、に、さん…) for most other contexts. Large numbers group by 万 (10,000) rather than 1,000 — this trips up most beginners.
cheatSheet: 0–10: ぜろ、いち、に、さん、し/よん、ご、ろく、なな/しち、はち、く/きゅう、じゅう
Japanese groups large numbers by 万 (10,000), not 1,000
Counter suffixes: ~枚 (flat things), ~本 (long thin things), ~冊 (books)
Age uses ~歳 (さい): にじゅっさい = 20 years old
Price: えん (円) — 300円 = さんびゃくえん
tips: Japanese numbers are logical — master 1-10 and the rest follows a pattern
Counters (e.g. 枚 for flat things, 本 for long things) are essential — learn them with nouns
Native Japanese numbers (ひとつ, ふたつ...) are used for counting up to 10 in everyday speech
Numbers in time and money use Chinese-derived readings (いち, に, さん...)

cards.back: 300 = さんびゃく (not さんひゃく). 600 = ろっぴゃく. 800 = はっぴゃく. Sound changes are required.
3,000 = さんぜん (not さんせん). 8,000 = はっせん (not はちせん).
Japanese counts in units of 10,000 (まん), not 1,000. So 100,000 = じゅうまん (10 x 10,000), 1,000,000 = ひゃくまん (100 x 10,000).

cards.front: 三百 is read as さん＿＿＿
八千 is read as は＿＿＿

meta.notes: This lesson introduces self-introduction vocabulary and the basic sentence structure of Japanese. Key pattern: [Noun] は [Noun] です (X is Y). Japanese does not conjugate verbs for person — です works for all subjects.
cheatSheet: は (wa) = topic marker — marks what you're talking about
です (desu) = is/am/are — polite sentence ender
わたし (watashi) = I, ぼく (boku) = I (male, casual)
〜じん (jin) = nationality: アメリカじん = American
〜ご (go) = language: にほんご = Japanese language
せんこう (senko) = major/field of study
tips: は (wa) marks the topic; が (ga) marks the subject — they overlap but differ in nuance
です (desu) is the polite copula — the u is often silent in natural speech
じゃないです is more casual than ではありません for negation
Sentence order in Japanese is Subject → Object → Verb (SOV)

cards.back: は (wa) marks the topic. です (desu) = 'is'. わたしはがくせいです。= I am a student. The subject is often omitted when clear from context.
Add か to the end of a statement to make it a question. がくせいです → がくせいですか？ No change in word order needed.
なん/なに = 'what'. せんこうはなんですか。= What is your major? なんじですか。= What time is it? Use なん before です, じ, ねんせい.
Connects two nouns: Noun1 の Noun2. にほんごのがくせい = a student of Japanese. たけしさんのでんわばんごう = Takeshi's phone number.

cards.front: Honorific titles: さん, くん, ちゃん
cards.back: さん: default polite (Mr./Ms.). くん: for boys/male subordinates. ちゃん: for children/close friends. Never use on yourself.

cards.front: せんせい vs. きょうし
cards.back: せんせい is used to address/refer to a teacher (honorific). きょうし is used to describe your own occupation modestly. Never call yourself せんせい.

cards.front: Why あなた is rarely used
cards.back: あなた (you) is considered rude in most contexts. Use the person's name + さん instead. E.g., たけしさんはがくせいですか。

cards.front: わたし＿＿がくせいです。(I am a student.)
せんこう＿＿なんですか。(What is [your] major?)
にほんご＿＿せんせい (a teacher of Japanese)
I am a student of economics at the University of Tokyo.
cards.back: とうきょうだいがくのけいざいのがくせいです。
cards.romaji: toukyou daigaku no keizai no gakusei desu.

cards.back: 4 o'clock = よじ (not よんじ). 7 o'clock = しちじ (not ななじ). 9 o'clock = くじ (not きゅうじ).

meta.notes: Shopping vocabulary and the これ/それ/あれ system for pointing at things. Also covers asking for prices and using the question particle か. Japanese questions use the same word order as statements — just add か at the end.
cheatSheet: これ = this (near me), それ = that (near you), あれ = that over there
〜をください = Please give me ~ / I'll take ~
いくらですか = How much is it?
か (ka) = question particle — attach to end of any statement
〜円 (en) = yen — always attach to the number
どれ = which one? どこ = where?
tips: これ/それ/あれ (this/that/that over there) depend on speaker and listener position
いくら (ikura) means "how much" — essential for shopping
Prices use the counter 円 (en/yen) — numbers stay the same, just add 円
ください (kudasai) = please give me — polite way to request something

cards.back: then . . . ; if that is the case
これ (this, near me) / それ (that, near you) / あれ (that over there) / どれ (which). Used as standalone pronouns: これはなんですか。
この/その/あの/どの + noun. These must be followed by a noun: このほんはいくらですか。(How much is this book?)
ここ (here) / そこ (there) / あそこ (over there) / どこ (where). としょかんはどこですか。= Where is the library?
Replaces は to mean 'also'. たけしさんはにほんじんです。みちこさんもにほんじんです。= Michiko is also Japanese.

cards.front: Noun negation: じゃないです
cards.back: X は Y じゃないです = X is not Y. Formal: じゃありません or ではありません. これはにくじゃないです。= This is not meat.
Added to seek confirmation (right?). いいてんきですね。= Nice weather, isn't it? Use when you expect agreement.
Added to convey new information or assert. このえいがはおもしろいですよ。= This movie is interesting, you know. Use sparingly to avoid sounding pushy.

cards.front: ください vs. おねがいします
cards.back: ください = 'please give me' (with を). おねがいします = 'please' (politer, used for requests/services). コーヒーをください。/ コーヒー、おねがいします。

cards.front: これ＿＿ペンです。(This is a pen.)
それはにく＿＿＿＿です。(That is not meat.)
たけしさんはにほんじんです。メアリーさん＿＿にほんじんですか。(Is Mary also Japanese?)
＿＿ノートはいくらですか。(How much is that notebook near you?)
としょかんは＿＿ですか。(Where is the library?)
This is not a Japanese book.
cards.back: これはにほんごのほんじゃないです。
cards.romaji: kore wa nihongo no hon janai desu.

cards.front: How much is that watch over there?
cards.back: あのとけいはいくらですか。
cards.romaji: ano tokei wa ikura desu ka.

cards.back: このかばんをください。
ゆうびんきょくはどこですか。

meta.notes: Covers verb conjugation into て-form, making invitations, and telling time. て-form is one of the most versatile verb forms in Japanese — it connects actions, makes requests (〜てください), and forms the progressive (〜ています). Learn the て-form rules early.
cheatSheet: て-form: Group 1 verbs follow consonant rules (く→いて, ぐ→いで, す→して...)
〜ませんか = Won't you ~? / Would you like to ~? (invitation)
〜ましょう = Let's ~ (suggestion)
時 (ji) = o'clock, 分 (fun/pun) = minute
前 (mae) = before/ago, 後 (go) = after/later
いつ = when? 何時 (nanji) = what time?
tips: Time expressions come before the verb in Japanese
に (ni) marks specific time points (3時に); で (de) marks duration context
ませんか is a polite invitation form — softer than a direct imperative
Days of the week follow a pattern: 月火水木金土日 (moon, fire, water, wood, gold, earth, sun)

cards.back: Drop る, add ます. たべる → たべます. みる → みます. ねる → ねます.
Change final u-sound to i-sound, add ます. いく → いきます. のむ → のみます. はなす → はなします.
する → します. くる → きます. These two must be memorized.
Replace ます with ません. たべます → たべません. いきます → いきません.

cards.front: べんきょうする → masu form

cards.back: Marks the direct object of a verb. コーヒーをのみます。= I drink coffee. おんがくをききます。= I listen to music.
Marks where an action takes place. カフェでコーヒーをのみます。= I drink coffee at a cafe. Don't confuse with に.
Marks destination with movement verbs. がっこうにいきます。= I go to school. Also: うちにかえります。
Read as 'e'. Interchangeable with に for direction. がっこうへいきます。= I go to school. Slightly softer/vaguer than に.
Marks specific time of event. にちようびにいきます。Use with specific times/days. Do NOT use with relative words like きょう, あした, まいにち.

cards.front: に with time: when to use/not use
cards.back: USE に: days of week (にちようびに), specific hours (さんじに), dates. DON'T use に: きょう, あした, まいにち, こんばん, しゅうまつ.
Verb (negative masu) + か = 'Won't you...?' / 'Shall we...?'. えいがをみませんか。= Won't you watch a movie (with me)?
よく (often) > ときどき (sometimes) > あまり + neg (not much) > ぜんぜん + neg (not at all). Place before the verb.

cards.front: コーヒー＿＿のみます。(I drink coffee.)
カフェ＿＿べんきょうします。(I study at a cafe.)
がっこう＿＿いきます。(I go to school.)
どようび＿＿えいがをみます。(I watch a movie on Saturday.)
わたしはコーヒーを＿＿＿＿のみません。(I don't drink coffee much.)
I usually eat breakfast at home.
cards.back: たいていうちであさごはんをたべます。
cards.romaji: taitei uchi de asagohan o tabemasu.

cards.front: Shall we go to a cafe on Saturday?
cards.back: どようびにカフェにいきませんか。
cards.romaji: doyoubi ni kafe ni ikimasen ka.

cards.front: I don't watch TV at all.
cards.back: ぜんぜんテレビをみません。

cards.front: I sometimes listen to music at school.
cards.back: ときどきがっこうでおんがくをききます。
cards.romaji: tokidoki gakkou de ongaku o kikimasu.

cards.front: What time do you get up every day?
cards.back: まいにちなんじにおきますか。

meta.notes: Focuses on describing locations, giving directions, and using the particle に for destination. Also introduces adjective conjugation (い-adj vs な-adj). These two adjective types behave very differently — especially in past tense and negative forms.
cheatSheet: に (ni) = direction/destination marker: がっこうに行く = go to school
で (de) = location of action: としょかんで読む = read at the library
い-adj negative: たかい → たかくない (not expensive)
な-adj negative: しずかな → しずかじゃない (not quiet)
い-adj past: たかい → たかかった (was expensive)
Location words: 上 (ue), 下 (shita), 右 (migi), 左 (hidari), 中 (naka)
tips: い-adjectives conjugate directly; な-adjectives need な before nouns
Past tense of い-adj: drop い, add かった (e.g. たのしい→たのしかった)
Past tense of な-adj/nouns: でした instead of です
が here often expresses "but" as a soft connector between clauses

cards.front: あります vs. います
cards.back: あります = existence of non-living things (books, buildings). います = existence of living things (people, animals). テーブルのうえにほんがあります。こうえんにねこがいます。

cards.front: Location pattern: Place に Thing が あります/います
cards.back: Describes what exists at a location. としょかんにがくせいがいます。= There are students at the library.

cards.front: Location pattern: Thing は Place に あります/います
cards.back: Describes where something is located. ほんはつくえのうえにあります。= The book is on the desk.
みぎ (right), ひだり (left), まえ (front), うしろ (back), なか (inside), うえ (on/above), した (under/below), となり (next to), あいだ (between), ちかく (nearby).
Affirmative past: がくせいでした (was a student). Negative past: がくせいじゃなかったです (was not a student).

cards.front: Past tense: Verb ～ました/～ませんでした
cards.back: Affirmative: たべました (ate). Negative: たべませんでした (did not eat).
Connects nouns: パンとコーヒー (bread and coffee). 'Together with': ともだちとえいがをみました (watched a movie with a friend).
Duration of time needs no particle. にじかんべんきょうしました。= I studied for two hours. NOT にじかんをべんきょうしました。

cards.front: Particle も replacing は/を but appending to に/で
cards.back: も replaces は and を: コーヒーもいいです。But appends to に and で: カフェにもいきます。としょかんでもべんきょうします。
たくさん can go before or after the noun + を: しゃしんをたくさんとりました = たくさんしゃしんをとりました。

cards.front: つくえの＿＿にほんがあります。(There is a book on the desk.)
こうえんにねこ＿＿います。(There is a cat in the park.)
きのうえいがを＿＿＿＿。(I watched a movie yesterday.)
ともだち＿＿レストランにいきました。(I went to a restaurant with a friend.)
きのうべんきょう＿＿＿＿＿＿。(I did not study yesterday.)
There is a bank next to the post office.
cards.back: ゆうびんきょくのとなりにぎんこうがあります。
cards.romaji: yuubinkyoku no tonari ni ginkou ga arimasu.

cards.front: I took a lot of pictures yesterday.
cards.back: きのうしゃしんをたくさんとりました。

cards.front: I waited for about one hour.
cards.back: いちじかんぐらいまちました。

cards.front: There is a cat between the desk and the chair.
cards.back: つくえといすのあいだにねこがいます。
cards.romaji: tsukue to isu no aida ni neko ga imasu.

cards.front: ぎんこうはとしょかんの＿＿にあります。(The bank is to the right of the library.)
ねこはテーブルの＿＿にいます。(The cat is under the table.)

cards.back: Features: tatami (straw mat flooring), fusuma (sliding doors), genkan (entryway where shoes are removed). Rooms measured in tatami mats (e.g., 6-mat room).
Japan has 16 national holidays. When a holiday falls on Sunday, the following Monday becomes a substitute holiday (振替休日). Golden Week is late April to early May.

meta.notes: Covers past-tense verbs, adjective conjugation in past tense, and travel vocabulary. This lesson also introduces the て-form for connecting sequential actions (verb て verb). Focus on how plain past forms differ between verb groups.
cheatSheet: Past tense (polite): 〜ました (affirmative), 〜ませんでした (negative)
い-adj past: おもしろい → おもしろかった (was interesting)
な-adj past: きれいな → きれいだった (was pretty)
〜てから = after doing ~: 食べてから行く = go after eating
〜たい = want to ~: 行きたい = want to go
もう = already, まだ = not yet, また = again
tips: て-form is the Swiss Army knife of Japanese — it connects actions and forms many patterns
て+います can mean an ongoing action OR a state resulting from an action
Transport: で marks the means (バスで = by bus); に marks the destination
Okinawa dialect (うちなーぐち) differs significantly from standard Japanese

cards.back: i-adj end in い: おもしろい, たかい. na-adj don't: げんき(な), しずか(な). EXCEPTION: きれい and きらい look like i-adj but are na-adj.
Drop い, add くないです. おもしろい → おもしろくないです. EXCEPTION: いい → よくないです.
Drop な, add じゃないです. げんきな → げんきじゃないです. しずかな → しずかじゃないです.
Drop い, add かったです. おもしろい → おもしろかったです. EXCEPTION: いい → よかったです.
Drop な, add でした. げんきな → げんきでした. きれいな → きれいでした.
Drop い, add くなかったです. おもしろい → おもしろくなかったです. EXCEPTION: いい → よくなかったです.
Drop な, add じゃなかったです. げんきな → げんきじゃなかったです.
i-adj directly modifies: おもしろいえいが (interesting movie). na-adj uses な: きれいなまち (beautiful town).
X は Y がすきです = X likes Y. Use が (not を) with すき/きらい. だいすき = love. だいきらい = hate.
Drop ます, add ましょう. たべます → たべましょう. いきます → いきましょう. Used for suggestions.

cards.front: このえいがは＿＿＿＿＿＿です。(This movie was not interesting.)
きのうのてんきは＿＿＿＿。(Yesterday's weather was good.)
わたしはさかな＿＿すきです。(I like fish.)
This town is quiet and beautiful.
cards.back: このまちはしずかできれいです。
cards.romaji: kono machi wa shizuka de kirei desu.

cards.back: きのうのテストはむずかしくなかったです。
cards.romaji: kinou no tesuto wa muzukashikunakatta desu.

cards.back: いっしょにひるごはんをたべましょう。
おもしろくなかったです
たのしくなかったです
むずかしくなかったです
かっこよくなかったです
げんきじゃなかったです
きれいじゃなかったです
しずかじゃなかったです
すきじゃなかったです
